column2: Harden audit-gate Worker against CF 1101 by adding three try/catch boundaries plus structured console.error logging
column3: PR #88's Oddkit Gauntlet runtime probe returned HTTP 500 with body `error code: 1101` (Cloudflare's raw "Worker threw exception" page), not the AuditGateDO's structured `{"error":"audit_failed",...}` envelope. The structural cause: the DO's own try/catch can only catch what happens INSIDE the DO fetch handler. A rejection from `stub.fetch(forwarded)` itself — cold-start hydration failure, DO storage fault, transport-layer issue, or any unhandled rejection between the Worker top-level and the DO body — escapes that envelope. Worker.fetch at the top level had NO try/catch, so the rejection bubbled all the way to CF, which emitted 1101. Fixed by adding four hardening sites: (1) global try/catch in Worker.fetch wrapping all route handling, (2) try/catch around `stub.fetch(forwarded)` in handleAuditGateTest, (3) console.error inside AuditGateDO.fetch's existing catch block, (4) explicit catch (in addition to finally) around the SSE streaming reader loop in runAuditSession. Each site logs structured context (url, method, head_sha, persona_uri, message, top-8-frames-of-stack) via console.error so Cloudflare Logpush / wrangler tail capture forensics for next time.

column2: Worker.fetch reshaped from inline export-default-object method into routeRequest helper plus envelope
column3: The default export keeps the same shape externally (`{async fetch(req, env, ctx)}`) but the body now does `try { return await routeRequest(req, env, ctx); } catch { log + envelope }`. The route-resolution logic moved verbatim into the standalone `async function routeRequest(...)`. No route handler changed. This refactor exists purely to add the catch boundary; the alternative (inlining a giant try at the top of fetch) would have created a visual barrier inside the routing logic and made it easier to accidentally drop the try later. A named helper is more legible.

column2: This fix CANNOT be exercised by the PR that contains it — validation gap disclosure per the new constraint
column3: Per ams://canon/constraints/validators-cannot-self-validate-their-own-fix-prs (encoded in PR #88, merged 2026-05-13), this PR modifies production-bound validators (handleAuditGateTest in index.ts + AuditGateDO in audit-gate.ts) and cannot exercise the fix on itself. The runtime probes on THIS PR call `https://ams.klappy.dev/audit-gate-test` which runs whatever is currently on `main` — the pre-fix version. The fix only becomes live when this PR merges and Cloudflare Workers Git integration auto-deploys. The first qualifying PR after this merges is where the 1101 hardening can be observed. Expected verdict matrix on the validation-event PR: Oddkit Gauntlet runtime probe completes with a real verdict (no raw 1101); if any path still throws, the response body should be a structured `{"error":"worker_unhandled_throw","message":"..."}` or `{"error":"audit_gate_do_transport_failed","message":"..."}` JSON, NOT the raw CF 1101 page. Cloudflare Logpush should also show structured console.error events at the named tags (worker_unhandled_throw, audit_gate_do_transport_throw, audit_gate_session_failed) for forensic review.

column2: The hardening is defense-in-depth, not a targeted fix for a known throw site
column3: I did not identify the specific code path that crashed in PR #88's Oddkit Gauntlet probe — without log access during the incident, the actual exception is opaque. The hardening makes the failure mode (raw CF 1101) impossible regardless of which throw site was at fault, AND adds the logging that the next incident needs to identify the actual root cause. If the underlying bug is upstream (Anthropic SSE event shape my parser doesn't recognize, oddkit MCP returning a payload that breaks the connector, etc.), this fix doesn't repair it — but it surfaces it as `{"error":"audit_failed","message":"<actual throw>"}` so the next session can iterate on the real failure mode instead of on the CF wrapping.

column2: Static analysis ruled out the easy explanations
column3: CPU time limit would be CF error 1102 (not 1101); memory exhaustion is unlikely at ~100KB streaming payloads on a 128MB-default worker; the SSE parser is robust against unknown event types and content_block types after Cursor agent's commit a68607b (trailing buffer flush) landed in PR #87. The streaming parser's try/finally with no catch propagates rejections cleanly to the DO's outer try/catch. The only path that explains 1101 is something throwing OUTSIDE the DO body — most likely `stub.fetch()` itself rejecting before the DO body runs, due to a DO-side cold-start or transport failure. This is the layer being hardened.

column2: Post-merge: the validation-event PR is where root cause becomes diagnosable
column3: Once this PR merges and auto-deploys, the next PR opened that touches `canon/**/*.md`, `writings/**/*.md`, or `journal/**/*.md` (which triggers the Oddkit Gauntlet runtime probe) becomes the validation event. If the probe still errors, the body will now be structured JSON with the actual exception message, and Cloudflare Logpush will have the stack trace at one of the four named log tags. That gives a concrete next investigation target. If the probe returns a real verdict, the underlying issue was indeed the CF envelope and the hardening is the complete fix. Standing flags unchanged: PAT and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in project instructions remain unrotated.
